citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
The facility failed to report an animal health concern to the attending veterinarian. An adult female domestic raccoon was
observed with significant hair loss along the dorsal and hindquarter regions. Live fleas were visibly crawling throughout
the hair coat. The facility representative stated that he bathed the animal in Dawn to get rid of the fleas. APHIS officials
asked if he contacted the attending veterinarian, and he responded, “no, I just bathed him in Dawn. That’s all that can be
done.”
Without veterinarian oversight, diagnoses and care methods may be inaccurate or inappropriate, potentially leading to
preventable illness or worsening animal health.
The licensee, in conjunction with the attending veterinarian, must use appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose,
and treat diseases and injuries, and must determine the availability of emergency, weekend, and holiday care. Correct
this day, April 30, 2026, forward.

The facility is not cleaning and sanitizing when necessary to satisfy generally accepted husbandry standards and
practices. A thick buildup of grime, urine and brown organic material was located throughout the indoor enclosure floor
and wooden perches housing a ring-tailed lemur.
Failure to replace contaminated structures, or properly cleaning these areas, can create a potential disease hazard for the
animals.
Hard surfaces with which nonhuman primates come in contact must be spot-cleaned daily and sanitized in accordance
with § 3.84 of this subpart to prevent accumulation of excreta or disease hazards. If the species scent mark, the surfaces
must be sanitized or replaced at regular intervals as determined by the attending veterinarian in accordance with
generally accepted professional and husbandry practices. Contaminated material must be removed or replaced whenever
raking and spot cleaning does not eliminate odors, diseases, insects, pests, or vermin infestation. All other surfaces of
housing facilities must be cleaned and sanitized when necessary to satisfy generally accepted husbandry standards and
practices.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
In the indoor enclosure housing a male lemur, an excessive number of fruit flies were observed flying in and around the
food bowl.
Pests are attracted to waste and unsanitary conditions. If they are present, they can spread diseases, contaminate food,
and create stressful or unsafe conditions for animals.
An effective program for control of insects, external parasites affecting nonhuman primates must be established and
maintained so as to promote the health and well-being of the animals and reduce contamination by pests in animal areas.

The facility is not cleaning primary enclosures frequently enough to reduce disease risks and odors. Two enclosures
housing one male and one female raccoon each contain more than a day’s accumulation of excreta, with dried urine
pooled on the inside and outside of the enclosure floor. The wire mesh, platforms, and ceiling have built up hair, grime,
and cobwebs. A strong urine and fecal odor is present in the room.
Unsanitary enclosures allow bacteria, parasites, mold, and viruses to build up. Regular cleaning reduces the risk of
respiratory issues, skin infections, and gastrointestinal illness. Because animals are highly sensitive to their environment,
accumulated waste, strong odors, or dirty bedding can cause stress, which in turn lowers immune function and increases
abnormal behaviors.
Excreta shall be removed from primary enclosures as often as necessary to prevent contamination of the animals
contained therein and to minimize disease hazards and to reduce odors.

The facility is not maintaining an effective pest control program. Large numbers of fruit flies were present in the food
receptacles and throughout two raccoon enclosures. A dead cockroach was found outside the male raccoon’s enclosure.
A female racoon was infested with fleas. Cobwebs were present on the enclosure wire mesh, and multiple spiderwebs
with egg sacs were observed on the ceiling above both enclosures.
Pests are attracted to waste and unsanitary conditions. If they are present, they can spread diseases, contaminate food,
and create stressful or unsafe conditions for animals. Effective sanitation practices and a comprehensive pest control
program help eliminate pests and reduce potential disease risks.
A safe and effective program for the control of insects, ectoparasites, and avian and mammalian pests shall be
established and maintained. Correct by May 5, 2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.
Additional Inspectors:
Colette Kirk El-Amin, Supervisory Animal Care Specialist n

narrative:
2.40(b)(2) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
The facility failed to report an animal health concern to the attending veterinarian. An adult female domestic raccoon was

The licensee, in conjunction with the attending veterinarian, must use appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, 
and treat diseases and injuries, and must determine the availability of emergency, weekend, and holiday care. Correct 
this day, April 30, 2026, forward. 
3.75(c)(3)  Repeat

The facility is not cleaning and sanitizing when necessary to satisfy generally accepted husbandry standards and

3.84(d)  Repeat

3.131(a)  Repeat

The facility is not cleaning primary enclosures frequently enough to reduce disease risks and odors. Two enclosures

Excreta shall be removed from primary enclosures as often as necessary to prevent contamination of the animals

3.131(d)

The facility is not maintaining an effective pest control program. Large numbers of fruit flies were present in the food
